messages.content:
I want to write a vba code that will copy only the rows that contain values from column E, column F, column G in sheet 'Reminders' starting from row 23
and paste into sheet 'Events' starting in the first empty cell of Column A, and pasting the data in the following order on the same row.
E from sheet 'Reminder' to C in sheet 'Events', F from sheet 'Reminder' to A in sheet 'Events', G from sheet 'Reminder' to  D in sheet 'Events'.
It will then move on to the next row and do the same.

Here's the VBA code that meets your requirements:

Sub CopyRemindersToEvents()
    Dim remindersSheet As Worksheet
    Set remindersSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Reminders")

    Dim eventsSheet As Worksheet
    Set eventsSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Events")

    Dim lastRow As Long
    lastRow = remindersSheet.Cells(Rows.Count, "E").End(xlUp).Row

    Dim i As Long
    For i = 23 To lastRow
        If Not IsEmpty(remindersSheet.Range("E" & i).Value) And _
           Not IsEmpty(remindersSheet.Range("F" & i).Value) And _
           Not IsEmpty(remindersSheet.Range("G" & i).Value) Then
            Dim destinationRow As Long
            destinationRow = eventsSheet.Cells(Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row + 1

            eventsSheet.Range("A" & destinationRow).Value = remindersSheet.Range("F" & i).Value
            eventsSheet.Range("C" & destinationRow).Value = remindersSheet.Range("E" & i).Value
            eventsSheet.Range("D" & destinationRow).Value = remindersSheet.Range("G" & i).Value
        End If
    Next i
End Sub

To use this code, first make sure that you have a sheet called "Reminders" and another sheet called "Events" in your workbook. Then, press Alt + F11 to open the Visual Basic Editor, and insert a new module. Paste the above code into the new module.

Finally, simply run the CopyRemindersToEvents procedure by clicking on Macros in the Excel ribbon, selecting CopyRemindersToEvents, and clicking Run. This will copy all the relevant rows from the Reminders sheet, and paste them in the Events sheet as specified.
How can I make a sheet wrap text when I activate the sheet

You can use the Worksheet_Activate event to wrap text for a sheet when it is activated. Here's an example VBA code:

```vb
Private Sub Worksheet_Activate()
    Cells.WrapText = True
End Sub
```

To use this code, right-click on the sheet that you want to wrap text for, select "View Code", and paste the above code into the module that opens. This code will automatically wrap text in all cells of the active sheet when you activate it.
How can I write a vba code that will insert the date in a cell indicating when I last clicked a specific button or when I last ran a specific vba code

You can use Application.OnTime method to set a scheduled time for a procedure to be executed at a later time. When the scheduled procedure is executed, you can update a specific cell with the current date using VBA. Here's an example VBA code:

```vb
Private Sub UpdateLastClickedDate()
    'Update the cell with the current date
    Range("A1").Value = Now()
End Sub

Private Sub Button1_Click()
    'Schedule UpdateLastClickedDate to execute in 10 seconds
    Application.OnTime Now + TimeValue("00:00:10"), "UpdateLastClickedDate"
End Sub
```

In the above example, Button1_Click is the procedure that will execute when a specific button is clicked. You can replace it with the name of the procedure that you want to execute. When the button is clicked, UpdateLastClickedDate is scheduled to execute in 10 seconds. This will give time for the procedure to finish executing before updating the date in the cell A1.

Alternatively, you can update the cell with the current date directly within the procedure. Here's an example VBA code:

```vb
Private Sub Button1_Click()
    'Update the cell with the current date
    Range("A1").Value = Now()
    
    'Code that the button executes
    '...
End Sub
```

In the above example, the procedure that is executed when the button is clicked updates the cell A1 with the current date before executing any further code.

Is it possible to show this pop up without indicating the column : For i = 2 To lastRow
If eventsSheet.Range("A" & i).Value >= DateToCheck And eventsSheet.Range("A" & i).Value <= EndDate Then
MatchCount = MatchCount + 1
Matches = Matches & vbNewLine & _
"Date: " & eventsSheet.Range("A" & i).Value & vbNewLine & _
"Column B: " & eventsSheet.Range("B" & i).Value & vbNewLine & _
"Column C: " & eventsSheet.Range("C" & i).Value & vbNewLine & _
"Column D: " & eventsSheet.Range("D" & i).Value & vbNewLine
End If
Next i

Yes, you can create a pop-up without specifying a column. One way to achieve this is to use a `For Each` loop to loop through all the cells in a specific column. Here's an example code:

```vb
Private Sub ShowPopUp()
    Dim eventsSheet As Worksheet
    Set eventsSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Sheet1")

    Dim DateToCheck As Date
    DateToCheck = Date
    
    Dim EndDate As Date
    EndDate = DateAdd("d", 7, DateToCheck)
    
    Dim MatchCount As Long
    Dim Matches As String

    'Loop through all cells in Column A
    Dim cell As Range
    For Each cell In eventsSheet.Range("A2:A" & eventsSheet.Cells(eventsSheet.Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row)
        If cell.Value >= DateToCheck And cell.Value <= EndDate Then
            MatchCount = MatchCount + 1
            Matches = Matches & vbNewLine & _
            "Date: " & cell.Value & vbNewLine & _
            "Column B: " & cell.Offset(0, 1).Value & vbNewLine & _
            "Column C: " & cell.Offset(0, 2).Value & vbNewLine & _
            "Column D: " & cell.Offset(0, 3).Value & vbNewLine
        End If
    Next cell
    
    'Show pop-up with matches
    If MatchCount > 0 Then
        MsgBox "Matches found:" & vbNewLine & Matches
    Else
        MsgBox "No matches found."
    End If
End Sub
```

In the above code, the `For Each` loop loops through all cells in Column A. The `cell` variable refers to the current cell in the loop, and you can use the `Offset` method to refer to cells in other columns relative to the current cell.

You can replace the `eventsSheet.Range("A2:A" & eventsSheet.Cells(eventsSheet.Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row)` with the range of cells you want to loop through. For example, you can use `eventsSheet.UsedRange` to loop through all used cells on the sheet.
What i meant is not showing the word Column B: Column C: Column D: in the pop up message

To remove the column letters from the pop-up message, you can simply remove the text `Column B:`, `Column C:`, and `Column D:` from the string concatenation. Here's the modified code:

```vb
Private Sub ShowPopUp()
    Dim eventsSheet As Worksheet
    Set eventsSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Sheet1")

    Dim DateToCheck As Date
    DateToCheck = Date
    
    Dim EndDate As Date
    EndDate = DateAdd("d", 7, DateToCheck)
    
    Dim MatchCount As Long
    Dim Matches As String

    'Loop through all cells in Column A
    Dim cell As Range
    For Each cell In eventsSheet.Range("A2:A" & eventsSheet.Cells(eventsSheet.Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row)
        If cell.Value >= DateToCheck And cell.Value <= EndDate Then
            MatchCount = MatchCount + 1
            Matches = Matches & vbNewLine & _
            "Date: " & cell.Value & vbNewLine & _
            cell.Offset(0, 1).Value & vbNewLine & _
            cell.Offset(0, 2).Value & vbNewLine & _
            cell.Offset(0, 3).Value & vbNewLine
        End If
    Next cell
    
    'Show pop-up with matches
    If MatchCount > 0 Then
        MsgBox "Matches found:" & vbNewLine & Matches
    Else
        MsgBox "No matches found."
    End If
End Sub
```

In the above code, the `cell.Offset(0, 1).Value` refers to the value in the cell one column to the right of the current cell (i.e. Column B), and similarly for `cell.Offset(0, 2).Value` and `cell.Offset(0, 3).Value`.

This code will show a message box with the date in Column A and the values in the three columns to the right of the date for each match found.

How can I write a vba code that checks all the cells in column C that have values, and if a duplicate is found, it pops up a message stating that duplicates have been found and it lists the cell address where the duplicates exist

You can use a Dictionary object in VBA to efficiently check for duplicates in a column. Here's an example code that checks all the cells in Column C for duplicates:

```vb
Sub CheckForDuplicates()
    Dim dataSheet As Worksheet
    Set dataSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Sheet1")
    
    Dim lastRow As Long
    lastRow = dataSheet.Cells(Rows.Count, "C").End(xlUp).Row
    
    Dim duplicatesDict As Object
    Set duplicatesDict = CreateObject("Scripting.Dictionary")
    
    Dim duplicateFound As Boolean
    duplicateFound = False
    
    Dim cell As Range
    For Each cell In dataSheet.Range("C2:C" & lastRow)
        If Not IsEmpty(cell.Value) Then
            If duplicatesDict.exists(cell.Value) Then
                duplicatesDict.Item(cell.Value) = duplicatesDict.Item(cell.Value) & ", " & cell.Address
                duplicateFound = True
            Else
                duplicatesDict.Add cell.Value, cell.Address
            End If
        End If
    Next cell
    
    If duplicateFound Then
        MsgBox "Duplicates found:" & vbNewLine & Join(duplicatesDict.items, vbNewLine)
    Else
        MsgBox "No duplicates found."
    End If
End Sub
```

In the above code, the `duplicatesDict` object is used to store the values in Column C for each cell that has a value. If a value is encountered that already exists in the dictionary, the current cell address is added to the string of existing cell addresses for that value.

After looping through all the cells in Column C, a message box is displayed listing all the values with duplicates, along with the cell addresses where the duplicates exist.

You can customize the message box text by modifying the "Duplicates found:" string, or by changing the format of the dictionary items in the Join function.

The code appears to be searching for individual values or letters. I want it to check if the first 10 letters of the cell or the first three words in a cell are duplicated as the first three words in another cell

To modify the code to check for duplicate first 10 letters of each cell, you can use the `Left` function to extract the first 10 letters from each cell, and use a dictionary to check for duplicates among the extracted strings. Here's the modified code:

```vb
Sub CheckForDuplicates()
    Dim dataSheet As Worksheet
    Set dataSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Sheet1")
    
    Dim lastRow As Long
    lastRow = dataSheet.Cells(Rows.Count, "C").End(xlUp).Row
    
    Dim duplicatesDict As Object
    Set duplicatesDict = CreateObject("Scripting.Dictionary")
    
    Dim duplicateFound As Boolean
    duplicateFound = False
    
    Dim cell As Range
    For Each cell In dataSheet.Range("C2:C" & lastRow)
        If Not IsEmpty(cell.Value) Then
            Dim cellKey As String
            cellKey = Left(cell.Value, 10)
            
            If duplicatesDict.exists(cellKey) Then
                duplicatesDict.Item(cellKey) = duplicatesDict.Item(cellKey) & ", " & cell.Address
                duplicateFound = True
            Else
                duplicatesDict.Add cellKey, cell.Address
            End If
        End If
    Next cell
    
    If duplicateFound Then
        MsgBox "Duplicates found:" & vbNewLine & Join(duplicatesDict.items, vbNewLine)
    Else
        MsgBox "No duplicates found."
    End If
End Sub
```

In the above code, the `cellKey` variable is set to the first 10 letters of each cell by using the `Left` function. The `duplicatesDict` object is used to keep track of the cell addresses for cells that have the same `cellKey`.

To modify the code to check for duplicate first three words in each cell, you can use the `Split` function to split each cell value into an array of words, and then extract the first three words to use as the `cellKey`. Here's the modified code:

```vb
Sub CheckForDuplicates()
    Dim dataSheet As Worksheet
    Set dataSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Sheet1")
    
    Dim lastRow As Long
    lastRow = dataSheet.Cells(Rows.Count, "C").End(xlUp).Row
    
    Dim duplicatesDict As Object
    Set duplicatesDict = CreateObject("Scripting.Dictionary")
    
    Dim duplicateFound As Boolean
    duplicateFound = False
    
    Dim cell As Range
    For Each cell In dataSheet.Range("C2:C" & lastRow)
        If Not IsEmpty(cell.Value) Then
            Dim words As Variant
            words = Split(cell.Value, " ", 3)
            
            Dim cellKey As String
            cellKey = words(0) & " " & words(1) & " " & words(2)
            
            If duplicatesDict.exists(cellKey) Then
                duplicatesDict.Item(cellKey) = duplicatesDict.Item(cellKey) & ", " & cell.Address
                duplicateFound = True
            Else
                duplicatesDict.Add cellKey, cell.Address
            End If
        End If
    Next cell
    
    If duplicateFound Then
        MsgBox "Duplicates found:" & vbNewLine & Join(duplicatesDict.items, vbNewLine)
    Else
        MsgBox "No duplicates found."
    End If
End Sub
```

In the above code, the `words` variable is set to an array of words in each cell by using the `Split` function. The `cellKey` variable is set to the first three words in each cell by concatenating the first three elements of the `words` array. The `duplicatesDict` object is used to keep track of the cell addresses for cells that have the same `cellKey`.
none of the vba codes to check for duplicates are working accurately

I'm sorry to hear that you're having trouble with the duplicate checking VBA codes. Can you provide more details about what is not working accurately and any error messages that you are encountering?

Without additional information, it's difficult to determine the root cause of the issue. Here are some potential issues that may be affecting the accuracy of the codes:

1. The VBA codes may not be treating text strings with different capitalization as duplicates. To avoid this, you can convert all the text values to upper or lower case before checking for duplicates.

2. The VBA codes may be treating cells with leading or trailing spaces as different values. To avoid this, you can use the `Trim` function to remove leading and trailing spaces from text values before checking for duplicates.

3. The VBA codes may not be accounting for other characters besides spaces in the text values. For example, a comma, period, or semicolon can make a text value appear different even if the words are the same. To avoid this, you can use regular expressions to clean up the text values before checking for duplicates.

If you can provide more information about the issue you're encountering, I'd be happy to help further.
All the cells in column C have text in them. The text is a minimum of one sentence long. some have up to three sentences in a cell

In that case, you can modify the VBA code to check for duplicate first three sentences in each cell by splitting each cell value by the period followed by a space (". "), and extracting the first three sentences as the `cellKey` for each cell. Here's the modified VBA code:

```vb
Sub CheckForDuplicates()
    Dim dataSheet As Worksheet
    Set dataSheet = ThisWorkbook.Sheets("Sheet1")
    
    Dim lastRow As Long
    lastRow = dataSheet.Cells(Rows.Count, "C").End(xlUp).Row
    
    Dim duplicatesDict As Object
    Set duplicatesDict = CreateObject("Scripting.Dictionary")
    
    Dim duplicateFound As Boolean
    duplicateFound = False
    
    Dim cell As Range
    For Each cell In dataSheet.Range("C2:C" & lastRow)
        If Not IsEmpty(cell.Value) Then
            Dim sentences As Variant
            sentences = Split(cell.Value, ". ")
            
            Dim cellKey As String
            If UBound(sentences) >= 2 Then
                cellKey = sentences(0) & ". " & sentences(1) & ". " & sentences(2) & "."
            ElseIf UBound(sentences) >= 1 Then
                cellKey = sentences(0) & ". " & sentences(1) & "."
            Else
                cellKey = sentences(0) & "."
            End If
            
            cellKey = Trim(cellKey)
            
            If duplicatesDict.exists(cellKey) Then
                duplicatesDict.Item(cellKey) = duplicatesDict.Item(cellKey) & ", " & cell.Address
                duplicateFound = True
            Else
                duplicatesDict.Add cellKey, cell.Address
            End If
        End If
    Next cell
    
    If duplicateFound Then
        MsgBox "Duplicates found:" & vbNewLine & Join(duplicatesDict.items, vbNewLine)
    Else
        MsgBox "No duplicates found."
    End If
End Sub
```

In the above code, the `sentences` variable is set to an array of sentences in each
